seed: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

transcript.content: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

It sounds like you’re worried that sharing might make others feel weighed down, so you keep your thoughts to yourself.  
What evidence do you have that people actually feel burdened when you talk to them?  
Can you think of a time when you shared something and the response was supportive or helpful?

I guess I just... I don't want to be a bother, you know? Like, I worry that my problems are too much for everyone else. There was that one time I talked to my friend about how I was feeling really anxious, and she seemed to understand, but then I felt guilty afterward for bringing it up. It's confusing.

It sounds like you’re worried that talking about your anxiety might overwhelm your friend. What evidence do you have that sharing your feelings is a burden? Have there been moments when your friend offered support or felt relieved to listen?

Yeah, I mean, she did say she was glad I opened up, but part of me still feels like I’m just dragging everyone down. I don’t want to be that person who always has problems. It’s like, I don’t know, I just want to be normal like everyone else, and I hate feeling like this.

It sounds like you’re worried that opening up makes you a burden. What moments have you noticed that suggest otherwise? How might you view the situation if you considered that sharing your feelings could also help others understand you better?

I guess there are times when I’ve shared things, and people have responded with kindness. Maybe it does help them see what I’m going through, but... I still worry. I mean, what if I just end up pushing them away instead? It's just really hard to shake off that feeling like I'm asking too much from others.

It sounds like you’re worried that sharing might push people away. What evidence do you have that this actually happens? Have there been moments when people stayed supportive or responded kindly after you opened up?

I don’t know, maybe I just focus too much on the few times it felt awkward. I mean, there have definitely been moments when friends were understanding and even offered to help. But then, I just keep replaying the times when I felt like I was burdening them. It’s like I can’t trust that they’ll always want to be there, you know?

It sounds like those moments of feeling like a burden are lingering in your mind. What evidence do you have that your friends would want to be there for you, and what evidence suggests they might not? Exploring both sides might help you see a fuller picture of the situation.

Yeah, I mean, I know they’ve been there for me before. Like, when I was going through that rough patch last year, they made an effort to check in on me, and that meant a lot. But then there's this nagging thought that maybe they were just being nice out of obligation. I guess I feel stuck between remembering the good moments and all those worries that creep in.

It sounds like you’re weighing both sides of that idea. What evidence do you have that their check‑ins were just an obligation, and what evidence might suggest they genuinely cared? Looking at both angles can help you see if there’s another way to view their support.

Yeah, I guess I can see what you're saying. I mean, on one hand, they seemed genuinely concerned and reached out without me having to say anything. But then I get these thoughts that maybe they just felt sorry for me or didn't know how to respond, you know? It’s just so easy to focus on those doubts. It feels safer to think they’re just being nice because they have

It sounds like you’re weighing whether their outreach was out of genuine concern or just a feeling of pity. What specific actions or words made you think they were just being nice? On the other hand, can you think of any moments that suggest they truly cared?

I think it was just... little things, like when they’d text me out of the blue or invite me to hang out when I was feeling down. But then I also remember moments where it felt almost forced, like they were just trying to fill a gap or didn’t really know what else to say. It’s confusing because I want to believe they care, but those doubts just keep whispering

It sounds like you’re weighing what feels like caring against moments that feel more like a gap‑filling gesture. What specific actions or words have made you feel genuinely cared for? And can you think of any moments that suggest the opposite? Exploring those details might help you see the full picture.

Yeah, I guess there were times when friends really listened, like when I opened up about my stress, and they didn’t rush me. They just sat with me, and it felt... comforting. But then I remember a couple of times when someone just kind of brushed off what I said or changed the subject quickly. Those moments definitely stick with me, and they make me second-guess everything else

It sounds like those moments where you felt brushed off have left a strong impression. What evidence do you have that this pattern applies to all your friendships? Are there other times when people have responded differently, and how might those experiences fit into the bigger picture?

I guess I don’t have evidence for all my friendships, but those moments just stand out so much. It’s like they replay in my head and drown out the good stuff. I mean, there have been times when people have really listened, like during that tough time with my job. They were so supportive. It’s just hard to hold on to those memories when the doubts feel so loud.

It sounds like you’re feeling unsure about the strength of your friendships. What specific moments or actions do you think show that they’re not solid, and can you also recall times when they were reliable or supportive? Looking at both sides might help you see a fuller picture of what’s actually happening.

I guess it’s just that contrast that gets to me. I think about how some friends really showed up for me, like when I was anxious leading up to that big presentation. They’d text me, tell me I could do it. But, then there were those moments, like when I tried to explain what I was feeling, and someone just changed the subject. It’s like those little things
